chapter: A Journey through States of Water

cards.text: The process of conversion of water into its vapour state is called evaporation.

Observation on a Rainy Morning
  • Aavi and Thirav notice water puddles in the playground during a rainy morning.

Puddles in the Evening
  • By evening, some water from the puddles disappears, sparking curiosity.

Seepage Hypothesis
  • Aavi considers that water may have seeped through the surface.

Surface Theory
  • Thirav disagrees, believing the water did not seep through the surface.

Evaporation Investigation
  • Activity 8.2 involves investigating how water disappears.

Water Vapour
  • Water changes into a gaseous state known as water vapour.

Invisible Water Vapour
  • Water vapour is not visible but can be detected through tiny steam droplets.

Steam Formation
  • When water is sprinkled on a hot pan, it converts to steam.

Steam Composition
  • Steam consists of water vapour, with some converting back into droplets.

Understanding Evaporation
  • The transformation of water into vapour is defined as evaporation.

State of Water
  • Water exists in liquid, solid, and gaseous states, with vapour being gaseous.

Water Disappearance
  • Observing where and how water disappears involves evaporation.

Role of Heat
  • Heat speeds up the conversion of water into its vapour form.

Practical Observation
  • By observing steam, one learns about the invisible transformation of water.

Concluding Theories
  • Both evaporation and steam provide insight into water's gaseous transition.
When the water vapour present in the air comes in contact with a cold surface, it forms water droplets. The process of conversion of water vapour into its liquid state is called condensation.

Making Lemonade Experience
Aavi, Thirav, and friends were involved in making lemonade.

Using Cold Water and Ice
Cold water was poured into a glass tumbler, followed by the addition of ice cubes.

Observation of Glass Surface
An interesting phenomenon was noticed on the outer surface of the glass tumbler after adding ice.

Understanding Condensation
Condensation occurs when water vapour converts to liquid upon contact with a cold surface.

Activity 8.3 Introduction
Activity 8.3: Let us experiment and observe the condensation process.

Experiment Explanation
Water vapour in the air forms water droplets when it comes into contact with a cold surface.

Description of Condensation
Conversion of water vapour to liquid is referred to as condensation.

Activity 8.4 Introduction
Activity 8.4: Let us measure the changes during condensation.

Experiment Details
Increased weight is observed on the digital weighing balance due to condensation.

How Condensation Occurs
Steps:
  • Water vapour present in the air.
  • Contacts cold surface of the tumbler.
  • Converts into water droplets.

Significance of Cold Surfaces
Cold surfaces trigger the conversion of water vapour to liquid droplets.

Impact on Weight Measurement
Due to condensation, the digital weighing balance shows an increased reading.

Phenomenon Observed
Water droplets form externally on the glass due to the cooling effect of ice.

Scientific Observation
Changing physical state due to temperature influences the environment.

Concept Recap
Condensation defined as the transition of water vapour into liquid when encountering a colder surface.

topics.topic: What are the different States of Water?

cards.text: Water is a substance that can be observed in three different states in our daily life. In the solid state, it exists as ice. On heating, the ice melts and gets converted into its liquid state. On further heating, water gets converted into its gaseous state.
Ice (solid state) retains its shape irrespective of the container in which it is placed while water takes the shape of the container. Ice does not flow or spread.
Water (liquid state) flows and changes its shape. Water does not have a fixed shape. It takes up the shape of the container in which it is kept, but the volume of water remains constant.

Water vapour (gaseous state) exhibits a property of spreading out in the entire available space. Gases do not possess a fixed shape. Water vapour exists even at room temperature; though it is invisible to us. It is present in the air around us.
States of Water Water exists in three states: solid, liquid, and gas.
Solid State: Ice Ice retains its shape regardless of the container.
Properties of Ice - Does not flow. - Maintains its shape.
Transition from Solid to Liquid Ice melts upon heating, transitioning into water.
Liquid State: Water Flowing, it changes shape based on its container.
Properties of Water - Adopts the shape of its container. - Retains constant volume.
Transition from Liquid to Gas Additional heating transforms water into water vapour.
Gaseous State: Water Vapour Spreads evenly in its surrounding space.
Properties of Water Vapour - Lacks fixed shape - Invisible at room temperature - Present naturally in the air.
Examples of Water Vapour Formation - Evaporation during clothes drying. - During mopping.
Other Substances in Various States - Substances such as wax, oil, and ghee exhibit solid, liquid, and gas states.
Examples of Solids Substances like ice are solid at room temperature.
Examples of Liquids - Water - Oil, similar to water in its flow characteristics.
Examples of Gases - Water vapour - Air components like oxygen and nitrogen.
Everyday Observations Changes of state are common around us in nature and daily activities.
States of Water: Water can be observed in three states: solid, liquid, and gas.

Solid State (Ice): Ice retains its shape regardless of the container.
  • Ice does not flow or spread.
Melting Process: On heating, ice melts into a liquid.

Liquid State (Water): Liquid water flows and takes the shape of its container.
  • Water does not have a fixed shape but a constant volume.
Evaporation Process: With further heating, liquid water becomes water vapor.
Gaseous State (Water Vapor): Spreads across all available space and doesn't have a fixed shape.
Presence of Vapors: Water vapor is present even at room temperature.
Invisibility of Vapors: Although water vapor is invisible, it's present in the air.
Evaporation Contribution: Daily activities like drying clothes and mopping floors add to airborne water vapor.
Other Exemplars: Substances such as wax, oil, and ghee also exhibit states similar to water.

Solid State Examples: Common solids beyond ice include:
  • Wood
  • Stone
  • Metal

Liquid State Examples: Beyond water, other liquids are:
  • Milk
  • Oil
  • Alcohol

Gaseous State Examples: Beyond water vapor, other gases are:
  • Oxygen
  • Hydrogen
  • Carbon Dioxide
Shape Characteristics: Solids have fixed shapes, liquids adjust shapes, gases occupy the available space.
Observational Instances: Ice, water, and vapor scenarios frequently appear in daily life observations.

topics.topic: How can we change the States of Water?

cards.text: The process of conversion of a solid into liquid state is called melting.
The process of conversion of liquid into solid state is called freezing.

Atmospheric Water Generator (AWG)
Collects drinkable water from humid air through condensation.
  • Similar to water drops forming outside a cold glass.

Condensation Process
Cooling air leads to condensation of water vapor.
  • Analogous to the formation of dew.

Ice Formation and Melting
Placing water in a cold environment forms ice.
  • Ice melts back into water when removed from the freezer.

Candles and Wax
Candle wax solidifies upon cooling.
  • Transition from liquid wax to solid when cooled.

State Changes in Substances
Substances change states due to heating or cooling.
  • Examples: Water freezing, wax solidifying.

Melting Process
Conversion of a solid to liquid.
  • Occurs when heating substances like ice.

Freezing Process
Conversion of liquid to solid.
  • Occurs when cooling substances like water.

Practical Example of Condensation
Condensation is seen on cold surfaces, similar to AWG.
  • Example: Water droplets on a cold glass.

Understanding AWG Functionality
Simulates natural condensation process to gather water.
  • Mimics natural phenomena observed in cold conditions.

Importance of State Changes
Vital in water collection and everyday phenomena.
  • Understanding these can assist in various applications.

topics.topic: How can Water be Evaporated Faster or Slower?

cards.text: On a rainy day, water evaporates slowly. If the amount of water in the air is already high (more humidity), water evaporates slowly.

Understanding Evaporation
Evaporation occurs when water is exposed to air. The greater the exposure, the faster the evaporation process takes place.

Surface Area Effect on Evaporation
If water is spread over a wide surface, such as a plate, it increases the area exposed to the air and speeds up the evaporation.

Humidity on Rainy Days
Rainy days have higher humidity levels because the air contains more water vapor. This saturation can affect how things dry outside.

Factors Affecting Evaporation
  • Surface area: Larger areas increase evaporation rates.
  • Humidity: High water vapor content in the air can slow down evaporation.

Practical Implications of Humidity
- High humidity can make clothes take longer to dry.
- It can also create a sticky feeling on the skin when outdoors.

topics.topic: How do Clouds give us Rain?

cards.text: The water from the ocean and the Earth’s surface evaporates into the atmosphere as vapour and returns as rain, hail or snow, ultimately flowing back to the oceans. This circulation of water is known as the water cycle.

Condensation Process
Condensation is the process where water vapor turns into liquid droplets, playing a crucial role in returning evaporated water to Earth's surface.

Formation of Clouds
As air rises, it cools. Cooler air leads to condensation, where water vapor forms droplets around dust particles, creating clouds.

Role of Dust Particles
Dust particles provide a surface for water vapor to condense upon, aiding in cloud formation.

Rain Formation
Small water droplets in clouds combine to form larger drops. When these get heavy, they fall as rain.

Water Vapor Behavior
Water vapor is lighter than air, causing it to rise similarly to lighter gases in gas balloons.

Activity 8.11: Understanding Water Cycle
Water from oceans and Earth's surface evaporates and returns as rain, completing the water cycle.

Water Availability
Only a fraction of Earth's water is usable by living organisms. Most is in oceans and not directly usable.

Increasing Water Demand
Growing populations increase water demand, leading to shortages in many regions.

Importance of Water Conservation
To address shortages, it's crucial to use water wisely and avoid wastage, ensuring clean water bodies.

State Changes of Water
Water can exist as a solid, liquid, or gas, and it changes states when heated or cooled.

Factors Influencing Evaporation
Evaporation rates are affected by exposed area, humidity, and air movement.

Evaporation's Cooling Effect
As water evaporates, it absorbs heat from its surroundings, resulting in a cooling effect.

Water Cycle Overview
The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the surface of the Earth.

Investigating Evaporation and Condensation
Methods like observation, questioning, reasoning, and experiments help understand evaporation and condensation processes.

Nature’s Treasures Chapter
This chapter will further explore water and its conservation strategies.
The process of conversion of water into its vapour state is called evaporation.
The process of conversion of water vapour into its liquid state is called condensation.
Water changes its state on heating or cooling.
Conditions which make the evaporation faster or slower are exposed area, humidity, air movement, etc.
Evaporation causes cooling effect.
The water vapour in the air condenses to form tiny droplets of water, which appear as clouds. Many tiny water droplets come together and fall down as rain, hail or snow.
We have used the process of observation, questioning, possible reason and experimenting to find out the concepts of evaporation and condensation.
